title: Primes in arithmetic progressions to large moduli III: Uniform residue classes

thm:1.1.summary: Uniform equidistribution of primes with a weak error term: for $q=q_1q_2$ with $q_1\le x^{1/10-3\delta}$, $q_2\le x^{4/10+4\delta}$ (so $q$ up to $x^{1/2+\delta}$), $\sum_{q_1}\sum_{q_2}\sup_{(a,q_1q_2)=1}|\pi(x;q_1q_2,a)-\frac{\pi(x)}{\phi(q_1q_2)}| \ll_C \delta\,\pi(x) + \frac{x(\log\log x)^2}{(\log x)^2}$ — equidistribution past the Bombieri–Vinogradov / GRH $1/2$ barrier.

thm:1.2.summary: Almost-uniform equidistribution for primes to moduli $q_1q_2q_3 = x^{1/2+\delta}$ in a triply-factored range, uniform over the inner residue classes — a stronger structured form of equidistribution beyond $1/2$.

thm:1.3.summary: Uniform equidistribution of a minorant: constructs $\rho(n)$ that lies below the primes, has nearly full mass $\sum_{n\le x}\rho(n) \ge (1-o(1))\pi(x)$, and equidistributes uniformly in progressions to moduli up to $x^{1/2+\delta}$ — the form directly usable as sieve input.

cor:1.4.summary: Primes in all progressions for almost-all moduli: for all but $\le x^{1/2+\delta}/(\log x)^A$ exceptional moduli $q\le x^{1/2+\delta}$ with a divisor in $[x^{2/5+\delta},x^{3/7}]$, $\pi(x;q,a)$ is asymptotic to $\pi(x)/\phi(q)$ for every coprime $a$ — the headline level-of-distribution beyond $1/2$.

def:1.summary: Siegel–Walfisz condition: a sequence $\alpha_n$ satisfies it if $|\sum_{n\sim N,\,n\equiv a\,(q),(n,d)=1}\alpha_n - \frac{1}{\phi(q)}\sum_{n\sim N,(n,dq)=1}\alpha_n| \ll_A \frac{N\tau(d)^{O(1)}}{(\log N)^A}$ — the equidistribution-to-small-moduli hypothesis the Type II estimates assume.

prop:5.1.summary: Main Type II estimate: for $QR=x^{1/2+\delta}$, $NM\asymp x$ in the stated ranges and sequences $\alpha_n,\beta_m$ with $\alpha_n$ satisfying Siegel–Walfisz, bounds the bilinear error in progressions — the core dispersion/Type-II input behind Theorem 1.1.

prop:5.2.summary: Type II estimate near $x^{2/5}$: the bilinear estimate in the triply-factored regime $Q_1Q_2Q_3=x^{1/2+\delta}$ used for the almost-uniform Theorem 1.2.

prop:5.3.summary: Type II estimate near $x^{1/2}$: the bilinear estimate valid up to $Q_1^7 Q_2^{12} < x^{4-10\epsilon}$, the boundary regime feeding Theorem 1.2.

prop:5.4.summary: Type I / triple-divisor estimate: for $QR=x^{1/2+\delta}$ and a factorization $N_1N_2N_3M\asymp x$ with $M$ small, controls the smooth-variable error — the complementary input to the Type II estimates.

lem:6.1.summary: Heath-Brown identity: decomposes $\Lambda(n)$ for $n\le 2x$ into $O_k(1)$ Type I and Type II bilinear sums of smooth variables — the combinatorial identity turning the prime indicator into dispersion-amenable form.

lem:6.2.summary: Reduction to a fundamental-lemma-type condition: produces 1-bounded sequences for which the Type estimates apply, replacing the raw equidistribution requirement.

lem:6.3.summary: Smooth partition of unity: smooth non-negative $\widetilde\psi_1,\dots$ giving a dyadic decomposition of the variable ranges in the bilinear sums.

lem:6.4.summary: Double divisor function estimate over intervals $\mathcal{I}_1,\mathcal{I}_2\subseteq[1,x]$, bounding the $\tau\times\tau$ correlation sums that arise in the dispersion.

lem:6.5.summary: Asymptotics for rough numbers: $\sum_{n<x}\mathbf{1}_{P^-(n)\ge z}$ for $x^\epsilon\le z\le x$, the Buchstab/Mertens count of integers with no small prime factor.

lem:6.6.summary: Upper-bound sieve: a sequence $\lambda_d^+$ supported on $d\le x^\epsilon$ with $|\lambda_d^+|\le 1$ majorizing the primes, used to dominate the prime sums.

lem:6.7.summary: Separation of variables: for $N_1,\dots,N_r\asymp x$ and coefficients $\alpha_{n_1,\dots,n_r}$, separates a multilinear sum into single-variable factors amenable to the Type estimates.

lem:6.9.summary: Type II terms: isolates and bounds the Type II bilinear contributions from the Heath-Brown decomposition at level $Q_1=x^{1/10-3\delta}$.

lem:6.11.summary: Extended Type II estimate: the bilinear estimate for $N\in[x^{2/5}/4,4x^{3/5}]$, $M\in[x/(2N),2x/N]$ — the widest Type II range, driving the main Proposition 5.1.

lem:10.1.summary: Poisson summation for a smooth dyadically-supported $f$, converting a residue-class sum into a dual sum — the harmonic-analysis input to the Type II estimates.

lem:10.2.summary: Summation with a coprimality constraint via Poisson, handling the $(n,d)=1$ conditions inside the bilinear sums.

cor:5.5.summary: Triple divisor function in progressions: for $Q^4R^3+Q^3R^4 < x^2/(\log x)^C$, $\sum_{q\le Q}\sum_{r\le R}\sup_{(a,qr)=1}|\sum_{n\le x,\,n\equiv a\,(qr)}\tau_3(n) - \frac{1}{\phi(qr)}\sum_{n\le x,(n,qr)=1}\tau_3(n)| \ll x/(\log x)^A$.

edges.note: Primes in almost-all moduli follows from the uniform weak-error equidistribution (Theorem 1.1).
Section 7's proof of Theorem 1.1 rests on the main Type II estimate (Proposition 5.1).
Theorem 1.1 also uses the triple-divisor / Type I estimate (Proposition 5.4).
The proof reduces the equidistribution requirement via the fundamental-lemma-type condition (Lemma 6.2).
The prime sums are dominated by the upper-bound sieve (Lemma 6.6).
Section 8's proof of Theorem 1.2 uses the Type II estimate near $x^{2/5}$ (Proposition 5.2).
Theorem 1.2 uses the Type II estimate near $x^{1/2}$ (Proposition 5.3).
Section 9 builds the minorant from the almost-uniform equidistribution (Theorem 1.2).
The minorant construction also draws on the uniform weak-error equidistribution (Theorem 1.1).
The $\tau_3$ equidistribution corollary is read off from Proposition 5.4.
The main Type II estimate assumes the Siegel–Walfisz condition on $\alpha_n$.
Proposition 5.2 assumes the Siegel–Walfisz condition.
Proposition 5.3 assumes the Siegel–Walfisz condition.
The main Type II estimate starts from the Heath-Brown decomposition of $\Lambda$ (Lemma 6.1).
Proposition 5.1 is driven by the extended Type II estimate (Lemma 6.11).
The dyadic decomposition uses the smooth partition of unity (Lemma 6.3).
The near-$x^{2/5}$ estimate uses the Type II terms bound (Lemma 6.9).
The near-$x^{1/2}$ estimate uses the extended Type II estimate (Lemma 6.11).
The triple-divisor estimate uses separation of variables (Lemma 6.7).
The Type I estimate uses Poisson summation (Lemma 10.1).
The triple-divisor estimate uses the double divisor function bound (Lemma 6.4).
The extended Type II estimate builds on the basic Type II terms bound (Lemma 6.9).
The Type II terms arise from the Heath-Brown decomposition (Lemma 6.1).
Bounding the Type II terms uses summation with a coprimality constraint (Lemma 10.2).
The coprimality-constrained summation is an application of Poisson summation (Lemma 10.1).
The reduction uses the rough-number asymptotics (Lemma 6.5).

main_results.description: Equidistribution of primes in APs to moduli beyond $x^{1/2}$: weak-error uniform (1.1), almost-uniform (1.2), minorant (1.3), and the almost-all-moduli corollary (1.4).

hypotheses.description: The Siegel–Walfisz condition the bilinear estimates assume on their input sequences.

type_II_estimates.description: The bilinear (Type II) and triple-divisor (Type I) estimates that power the equidistribution theorems, across the $x^{2/5}$, $x^{1/2}$ and main regimes.

combinatorial_decomposition.description: The Heath-Brown identity decomposing $\Lambda$, the smooth partition of unity, separation of variables, and the reduction to a fundamental-lemma condition.

preparatory_sieve.description: Divisor-function and rough-number estimates and the upper-bound sieve majorant.

type_II_machinery.description: The Type II term bounds and the extended Type II estimate over the widest range.

harmonic_analysis.description: Poisson summation and coprimality-constrained summation, the analytic input to the bilinear estimates.

divisor_corollary.description: Equidistribution of the triple divisor function $\tau_3$ obtained from the Type I estimate.
